Header Image Credit: FOX Image Collection via Getty Images (© 2015 FOX), accessed via NBC.

6 min read
Just now

--

“Cool. Cool cool cool.”
If you’ve watched Brooklyn Nine-Nine, you probably read that in Jake Peralta’s voice.
That’s what makes great sitcoms so memorable. Every character has their own rhythm, vocabulary, and personality. Captain Holt can make a single sentence sound like an official government announcement. Rosa somehow says more with three words than most people do with thirty. Boyle has never met an awkward silence he couldn’t fill, and Gina… well, Gina lives in her own universe.
But it got me wondering.
If we stopped watching Brooklyn Nine-Nine as a sitcom and started looking at it as a dataset, would those personalities still show up?
As data analysts, we’re used to working with sales reports, customer data, and spreadsheets full of numbers. They’re useful, but data can come from almost anywhere. Sometimes the most interesting datasets aren’t business reports at all. Sometimes they’re thousands of lines of dialogue from one of your favorite TV shows.
So I loaded a Brooklyn Nine-Nine dialogue dataset into Python, cleaned it, engineered a few features, and started asking questions.
Some results were exactly what I expected.
Others made me laugh.
Let’s investigate.

The Dataset
The dataset is surprisingly simple. Every row contains only two columns:

• Character Name

• Dialogue
After cleaning the data by removing duplicates, handling missing values, standardizing character names, and creating a few extra features like word counts, question counts, and exclamation mark counts, it was ready for analysis.
Sometimes that’s all you need.
A good question is often more important than a complicated dataset.

Who Really Talks the Most?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let’s start with the easiest question.
Who actually speaks the most?
Jake Peralta takes first place, followed by Captain Holt and Amy Santiago.
Honestly…
Are we shocked?
Not even a little.
The show revolves around Jake, so seeing him dominate the conversation isn’t exactly breaking news. What surprised me more was Holt finishing second. Despite his calm, emotionless delivery, he’s involved in far more conversations than I realized. Amy rounding out the top three also makes perfect sense considering she’s usually solving a case, organizing the precinct, or cleaning up one of Jake’s “brilliant” ideas.
Sometimes the data simply confirms what fans have known all along.

Who Has the Longest Average Dialogue?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This was the first result that completely fooled me.
Before running the analysis, I was convinced Holt or Boyle would top the list. Holt loves delivering long, perfectly structured speeches, while Boyle somehow finds a way to turn every answer into a five-minute story.
Instead…
The winners were Mrs. Patterson, Bunder, M.E., and Flute Man.
I actually laughed when I saw the output.
It turns out these characters appear so rarely that when they do speak, it’s often one long block of dialogue. That’s the danger of averages. Without context, they can tell a very different story than you’d expect.
Sometimes the weirdest results end up teaching the best lessons.

Who Has the Richest Vocabulary?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Unlike the previous chart, this one felt exactly right.
Jake once again takes first place, followed by Holt, Amy, and Charles.
Jake simply speaks so much that it’s natural he’d accumulate the largest collection of unique words. Holt securing second place, however, says a lot about how he’s written. His dialogue is formal, precise, and surprisingly varied, making his vocabulary much richer than many other characters.
Charles also sneaks into the top four, which honestly feels appropriate. Whether he’s passionately discussing food, detective work, or Nikolaj (it’s Nikolaj), Boyle always finds new ways to express himself.

Who Asks the Most Questions?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Being a detective means asking questions for a living, so I expected the main detectives to dominate this category.
Jake finishes first yet again, followed by Holt, Amy, and Rosa.
The results almost perfectly match their personalities. Jake constantly throws out ideas, Amy wants every possible detail before making a decision, Holt asks direct questions that immediately get to the point, and Rosa… well, Rosa only asks questions when she genuinely wants an answer.
It’s a simple metric, but it captures something surprisingly human.

Who Uses the Most Exclamation Marks?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I never thought I’d analyze punctuation for a blog post.
Yet here we are.
Jake once again claims first place, followed by Charles and Terry.
Honestly, this chart might be the least surprising of them all.
Jake approaches every case like it’s the greatest adventure of his life. Charles somehow manages to be excited about literally everything, and Terry… Terry loves exclamation marks.
Apparently punctuation has personality too.

The Longest Dialogue
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One of my favorite parts of the project was finding the longest individual pieces of dialogue.
The longest speech belongs to Gina, followed by two entries from Jake.
Again…
That feels incredibly on-brand.
Gina has never believed in using fewer words when dramatically more words were available, and Jake has a remarkable ability to turn even the simplest conversation into a full performance.
Reading these dialogues outside the episodes was surprisingly entertaining. Without the music, camera cuts, or reactions from the rest of the squad, you start appreciating just how distinctive every character’s writing really is.

Jake vs. Holt: A Tale of Two Word Clouds
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Jake’s word cloudPress enter or click to view image in full size

holt’s word cloudThis comparison ended up being one of my favorite visualizations.
Jake’s most common words include captain, okay, oh, right, detective, think, sorry, and case. Even without hearing his voice, you can almost picture him sprinting through the precinct while apologizing for something he definitely caused.
Captain Holt’s word cloud tells an entirely different story.
Words like Peralta, detective, case, Santiago, officer, good job, and even his iconic “Bup, bup.” immediately stand out. It’s formal, structured, and unmistakably Holt.
What I love most is that if you removed the character names from the dataset, I’d still have a pretty good chance of guessing which word cloud belonged to which person.
That’s good writing.
And apparently, it’s measurable.

Final Thoughts
When I started this project, I expected a fun afternoon of exploring a sitcom with Python.
I didn’t expect the data to reflect the characters so accurately.
Jake really does dominate conversations. Holt’s vocabulary really is more diverse than most of the precinct. Charles is exactly as enthusiastic as you’d imagine, and even something as small as an exclamation mark can reveal a surprising amount about a character’s personality.
That’s what I love about data analysis.
It encourages you to look at familiar things from completely different angles.
A spreadsheet becomes a story.
Dialogue becomes data.
And a sitcom becomes something you can actually measure.
The next time someone tells you data analysis is only useful for business reports and sales dashboards, show them this project.
Because sometimes the most interesting insights come from the Nine-Nine.
Nine-Nine!
